corpus_meta.name: FSOS Compliance Intelligence — Seed Knowledge Corpus
corpus_meta.purpose: Grounding corpus for the NIGO analysis + note-authoring engine. Every chunk is tagged with authority_type (the tier hierarchy) and section_ref (for precise citation). Load into knowledge_chunks; embed chunk_text.
corpus_meta.verification_note: FINRA/SEC/state text below is paraphrased-for-index with section_refs to the authoritative source. BEFORE any chunk is used to ground an external NIGO response, load the verbatim rule text from the primary source and set verbatim=true. Chunks are marked verbatim=false until you do.
corpus_meta.critical_gap: FFS_PROCEDURE and CARRIER_REQUIREMENT chunks are STUBS. The system cannot cite an FFS manual or carrier rule it has never seen. You must upload the actual FFS compliance manual, WSPs, FCB bulletins, and carrier submission guides to populate these tiers.

chunks.title: 2330 — Reasonable basis to believe suitability (disclosure of features)
chunks.chunk_text:
Recommendation requirements
A member or associated person may not recommend a purchase or exchange unless there is a reasonable basis to
believe the customer has been informed of material features, would benefit from relevant annuity features, and the
contract, subaccounts, riders, and exchange as a whole are suitable. For an exchange, the analysis must consider
surrender charges, a new surrender period, loss of existing benefits, increased fees, product enhancements, and
exchanges within the prior 36 months.
Customer information
Before recommending a purchase or exchange, reasonable efforts must be made to obtain age, income, financial
situation and needs, investment experience and objectives, intended use, time horizon, existing assets including
insurance holdings, liquidity needs, liquid net worth, risk tolerance, tax status, and other relevant information.
Documentation and transmission
The recommending associated person must document and sign the required determinations and promptly transmit a
complete and correct application package to an office of supervisory jurisdiction.
Principal review
Before transmission to the insurer for processing, and no later than seven business days after the OSJ receives a
complete and correct package, a registered principal must approve or reject the recommendation and document and sign
the determination.
Supervision and training
The member must maintain specific written supervisory procedures, surveillance for potentially inappropriate exchange
rates, corrective measures, and documented training for associated persons and principals.
Exchange inquiry
Members must determine whether the customer had an exchange at the firm and make reasonable efforts to ascertain
exchanges at other broker-dealers during the preceding 36 months, documenting the inquiry and response.
chunks.paraphrase_backup: Before recommending the purchase or exchange of a deferred variable annuity, the associated person must have a reasonable basis to believe the customer has been informed of the material features of the annuity, including surrender charges, potential tax penalties, mortality and expense fees, investment advisory fees, potential charges for and features of riders, insurance and investment components, and market risk.

chunks.title: 2330 — Exchange-specific suitability factors
chunks.chunk_text:
For an exchange, the analysis must consider
surrender charges, a new surrender period, loss of existing benefits, increased fees, product enhancements, and
exchanges within the prior 36 months.
Customer information
Before recommending a purchase or exchange, reasonable efforts must be made to obtain age, income, financial
situation and needs, investment experience and objectives, intended use, time horizon, existing assets including
insurance holdings, liquidity needs, liquid net worth, risk tolerance, tax status, and other relevant information.
Documentation and transmission
The recommending associated person must document and sign the required determinations and promptly transmit a
complete and correct application package to an office of supervisory jurisdiction.
Principal review
Before transmission to the insurer for processing, and no later than seven business days after the OSJ receives a
complete and correct package, a registered principal must approve or reject the recommendation and document and sign
the determination.
Supervision and training
The member must maintain specific written supervisory procedures, surveillance for potentially inappropriate exchange
rates, corrective measures, and documented training for associated persons and principals.
Exchange inquiry
Members must determine whether the customer had an exchange at the firm and make reasonable efforts to ascertain
exchanges at other broker-dealers during the preceding 36 months, documenting the inquiry and response.
governs_nigo_patterns: loss of existing benefits not documented
chunks.paraphrase_backup: For an exchange of a deferred variable annuity, the associated person must consider whether: the customer would incur a surrender charge, be subject to a new surrender period, lose existing benefits (such as death, living, or other contractual benefits), or be subject to increased fees or charges; the customer would benefit from product enhancements and improvements; and the customer has had another exchange within the preceding 36 months.

chunks.title: 2330 — Suitability profile factors that must be considered
chunks.chunk_text:
Customer information
Before recommending a purchase or exchange, reasonable efforts must be made to obtain age, income, financial
situation and needs, investment experience and objectives, intended use, time horizon, existing assets including
insurance holdings, liquidity needs, liquid net worth, risk tolerance, tax status, and other relevant information.
Documentation and transmission
The recommending associated person must document and sign the required determinations and promptly transmit a
complete and correct application package to an office of supervisory jurisdiction.
Principal review
Before transmission to the insurer for processing, and no later than seven business days after the OSJ receives a
complete and correct package, a registered principal must approve or reject the recommendation and document and sign
the determination.
Supervision and training
The member must maintain specific written supervisory procedures, surveillance for potentially inappropriate exchange
rates, corrective measures, and documented training for associated persons and principals.
Exchange inquiry
Members must determine whether the customer had an exchange at the firm and make reasonable efforts to ascertain
exchanges at other broker-dealers during the preceding 36 months, documenting the inquiry and response.
chunks.paraphrase_backup: The recommendation must be suitable considering the customer's age, annual income, financial situation and needs, investment experience, investment objectives, intended use of the deferred variable annuity, investment time horizon, existing assets, liquidity needs, liquid net worth, risk tolerance, tax status, and such other information used or considered reasonable.

chunks.title: 2330 — Principal review standard and trigger
chunks.chunk_text:
Principal review
Before transmission to the insurer for processing, and no later than seven business days after the OSJ receives a
complete and correct package, a registered principal must approve or reject the recommendation and document and sign
the determination.
Supervision and training
The member must maintain specific written supervisory procedures, surveillance for potentially inappropriate exchange
rates, corrective measures, and documented training for associated persons and principals.
Exchange inquiry
Members must determine whether the customer had an exchange at the firm and make reasonable efforts to ascertain
exchanges at other broker-dealers during the preceding 36 months, documenting the inquiry and response.
chunks.paraphrase_backup: Prior to transmitting a customer's application for a deferred variable annuity to the issuing insurance company, a registered principal must review and determine whether he or she approves of the recommended transaction. The principal reviews within seven business days of the firm receiving a COMPLETE and CORRECT application package. The trigger is a complete and correct package; the seven business days is a ceiling, not a floor.

chunks.title: Reg BI — Care Obligation
chunks.chunk_text:
requiring that a broker-dealer exercise reasonable diligence, care, and skill to: (1) understand the 
risks, rewards and costs of a recommendation; (2) have a reasonable basis to believe that the 
recommendation is in the best interest of a particular retail customer, based on the retail 
customer’s investment profile, and that the recommendation does not place the broker-dealer’s 
interest ahead of the retail customer’s interest; and (3) have a reasonable basis to believe that a 
series of transactions is in the best interest of the retail customer and does not place the interest 
of the broker-dealer ahead of the retail customer’s interests. Regulation Best Interest imposes a 
duty of care that enhances existing suitability obligations (as discussed further below). It also 
includes a requirement under the Care Obligation to specifically address the risk that a broker-
dealer’s transaction-based recommendations and compensation could result in a series of 
recommendations that are not in the best interest or a retail customer—a “churning” risk unique 
to the broker-dealer model of providing recommendations and resulting transaction-based 
compensation. 
Regulation Best Interest also includes a requirement under the Conflict of Interest 
Obligation for broker-dealers to establish, maintain, and enforce written policies and procedures 
reasonably designed to (1) mitigate conflicts of interest at the associated person level, (2) 
specifically address the conflicts of interest presented when broker-dealers place material 
limitations on the securities or products that may be recommended (i.e., only make 
recommendations of proprietary or other limited range of products), and (3) eliminate sales 
contests, bonuses, and non-cash compensation that are based on the sales of specific securities or 
specific types of securities within a limited period of time. The conflicts of interest associated 
with incentives at the associated person level and limitations on the securities or products that 

62 
 
may be recommended to retail customers have raised particular concerns in the context of the 
broker-dealer, transaction-based relationship. Accordingly, the Commission believes specific 
disclosure and additional mitigation requirements are appropriate to address those conflicts. 
Sales contests, sales quotas, bonuses and non-cash compensation that are based on the sales of 
specific securities within a limited period of time create high-pressure situations for associated 
persons to increase the sales of specific securities or specific types of securities within
chunks.paraphrase_backup: The Care Obligation requires that the broker, in making a recommendation, exercise reasonable diligence, care, and skill to: (A) understand potential risks, rewards, and costs of the recommendation and have a reasonable basis to believe it could be in the retail customer's best interest; (B) have a reasonable basis to believe the recommendation is in the best interest of the particular retail customer based on that customer's investment profile; and (C) have a reasonable basis to believe that a series of recommended transactions is not excessive. The best-interest basis must not place the broker's interest ahead of the customer's.

chunks.title: Reg BI — Reasonably available alternatives
chunks.chunk_text:
customer, a broker-dealer generally should consider reasonably available alternatives offered by 
the broker-dealer. It is our view that such a consideration is an inherent aspect of making a “best 
interest” recommendation, and is a key enhancement over existing broker-dealer suitability 
 
 
636 As noted and further reiterated below, a broker-dealer will not be required to recommend 
the single “best” of all possible alternatives that might exist, in part because many 
different options may in fact be in the retail customer’s best interest. See infra footnote 
640 and accompanying text. 

284 
 
obligations, which do not necessarily require a comparative assessment among such 
alternatives.637 Similarly, this concept has been applied in the context of guidance regarding 
suitability and heightened supervision of complex products, stating that when broker-dealers are 
recommending complex or costly products, they should first consider whether less complex or 
costly products could achieve the same objectives for their retail customers.638 
In terms of conducting such an evaluation, a broker-dealer does not have to conduct an 
evaluation of every possible alternative, either offered outside of the firm (such as where the firm 
offers only proprietary or other limited range of products) or available on the firm’s platform. 
We appreciate commenter concerns about the impracticality and potential impossibility of such a 
 
 
637 While enforcement actions and related guidance may be construed as interpreting the 
suitability obligation to include a consideration of available alternatives, it is generally 
limited to certain circumstances, such as recommendations of mutual funds with different 
share classes or recommendations of complex or costly products. See In re Application 
of Raghavan Sathianathan, Exchange Act Release No. 54722 at 21 (Nov. 8, 2006); In the 
Matter of Wendell D. Belden, 56 S.E.C. 496 (2003); FINRA Regulatory Notice 12-03. 
See also FINRA 2018 Letter; MSRB Rule G-42 (requiring a municipal advisor to inform 
its municipal entity or obligated person client whether it has investigated or considered 
other reasonably feasible alternatives to the recommended municipal securities 
transaction). 
Thus, although certain enforcement actions and guidance contemplate a consideration of 
available alternatives under certain situations, it not a general expectation. Nevertheless, 
such statements serve as an example and evidence that the concept is not unfamiliar to 
broker-dealers. 
638 See FINRA Regulatory Notice 12-03 (“For example, registered re
governs_nigo_patterns: single fund family vs multi family
chunks.paraphrase_backup: As part of the Care Obligation, the broker should consider reasonably available alternatives offered by the firm as part of having a reasonable basis to believe the recommendation is in the customer's best interest. The broker need not evaluate every possible alternative, nor recommend the single 'best' product, but must consider reasonable alternatives and should be able to explain why the recommendation was selected over them.

chunks.title: 2111 — Suitability
chunks.chunk_text:
Core obligation
A member or associated person must have a reasonable basis to believe a recommended securities transaction or
investment strategy is suitable for the customer based on reasonable diligence concerning the customer’s investment
profile.
Investment profile
The profile includes age, other investments, financial situation and needs, tax status, objectives, experience, time
horizon, liquidity needs, risk tolerance, and other disclosed information.
Three components
The rule identifies reasonable-basis suitability, customer-specific suitability, and quantitative suitability.
Reg BI limitation
Rule 2111 does not apply to recommendations subject to Exchange Act Rule 15l-1, Regulation Best Interest.
chunks.paraphrase_backup: A member or associated person must have a reasonable basis to believe that a recommended transaction or investment strategy is suitable for the customer, based on the information obtained through reasonable diligence to ascertain the customer's investment profile, including age, other investments, financial situation and needs, tax status, investment objectives, investment experience, time horizon, liquidity needs, and risk tolerance.

chunks.section_ref: Rule 2342 / NTM 95-80 lineage; mutual fund breakpoint guidance
chunks.title: Breakpoints / Rights of Accumulation / Letter of Intent
chunks.chunk_text:
A member may not sell investment-company shares in dollar amounts just below a breakpoint in order to share in the
higher sales charges applicable below the breakpoint.
FINRA considers the facts and circumstances, including whether records demonstrate execution under a bona fide
asset-allocation program designed to meet diversification needs and investment goals and whether customers were told
they might not qualify for otherwise available breakpoint reductions.
chunks.leverage_note: This chunk is the direct bounce for the 'one fund family or decline' NIGO. The rule protects the client's discount; it does not mandate consolidation. A blanket 'one family or decline' is not supported by this FINRA rule — it is at most FFS_PROCEDURE. Retrieval here should surface the gap.
chunks.paraphrase_backup: Breakpoint discounts reduce front-end sales charges as investment size increases within a single fund family, via Rights of Accumulation (aggregating related holdings) and Letters of Intent. The regulatory concern is breakpoint SELLING abuse: structuring transactions to DEPRIVE a customer of a breakpoint discount they would otherwise qualify for, typically to preserve or increase commission. The rules do NOT prohibit investing across multiple fund families when the customer is informed of and understands the forgone breakpoint discount and the multi-family structure serves the customer's documented objectives.

chunks.title: 2010 — Standards of Commercial Honor
chunks.chunk_text:
A member, in the conduct of its business, shall observe high standards of commercial honor and just and equitable
principles of trade.
chunks.paraphrase_backup: A member, in the conduct of its business, shall observe high standards of commercial honor and just and equitable principles of trade. This rule underlies enforcement of signature integrity: forging or falsifying a customer signature, including via electronic signature platforms, violates Rule 2010 and does not require intent to defraud.

chunks.title: 4511 — Books and Records
chunks.chunk_text:
Members must make and preserve books and records required by FINRA rules, the Exchange Act, and applicable
Exchange Act rules.
FINRA books and records with no otherwise specified retention period must be preserved for at least six years.
Records required under FINRA rules must be preserved in a format and medium compliant with Exchange Act Rule
17a-4.
chunks.paraphrase_backup: Members must make and preserve books and records as required under FINRA rules, the Exchange Act, and applicable Exchange Act rules. Record integrity — including accurate, non-altered documentation and proper e-signature audit trails — falls under this obligation together with Rule 2010.

chunks.title: RN 22-18 — Digital signature forgery and falsification
chunks.chunk_text:
supervise for customer, colleague, and supervisor signature forgery or falsification through
third-party digital-signature platforms.
Regulatory framework
Forgery or falsification may violate Rule 2010. Where the affected document is a firm book or record, Rule 4511 also
applies because required records must be accurate. Rule 3110 requires a supervisory system reasonably designed for
compliance and requires firms to respond to red flags.
Reported detection methods
FINRA described customer complaints, audit-trail reviews, email reviews, IP-address analysis, discrepancies between
signer location and customer residence, use of representative-controlled email addresses, and identical
representative/customer IP addresses as potential indicators.
Operational implication
Digital-signature controls should preserve completion certificates and audit trails and support exception review for
anomalous email, IP, location, timing, and device data.
chunks.leverage_note: HIGHEST PERSONAL RISK. Enforcement, not just NIGO. If a case file shows a client signing from a rep-domain email, flag it as enforcement risk and route to compliance principal — do not treat as a routine correction.
chunks.paraphrase_backup: FINRA reminded firms of obligations to supervise for forgery and falsification of signatures via third-party digital signature platforms (Rules 2010, 4511, 3110). Red flags firms identified include: customer signatures originating from an email address associated with the representative; a customer signing IP address matching the representative's or inconsistent with the customer's address on file; documents sent to a representative-associated or outside-business email. Such conduct violates Rule 2010 and does not require intent to defraud.

chunks.section_ref: 2024 Annual Regulatory Oversight Report — Variable Annuities, Effective Practices
chunks.title: 2024 Report — Detailed, non-replicated exchange rationales
governs_nigo_patterns: why the customer desires this feature
chunks.leverage_note: This is the objective standard behind most 'not good enough' NIGOs. It is finite and listable. A note that hits every element here satisfies the standard; a reviewer who NIGOs it anyway must name the specific missing element.
chunks.paraphrase_backup: Effective practice: confirm that registered representatives' — and where applicable supervisory principals' — written rationales for variable annuity exchanges address the specific circumstances FOR EACH CUSTOMER and do NOT replicate rationales provided for other customers; and require supervisory principals to VERIFY the information in these rationales, including product fees, costs, rider benefits, and existing product values. Data considered in exchange review includes: living/death benefit value forfeited, surrender charges incurred, source of funds, share class, riders, issuer and purchase date of the exchanged policy, and any prior exchange within 36 months.

chunks.section_ref: 2024 Annual Regulatory Oversight Report — Variable Annuities, Findings
chunks.title: 2024 Report — Data quality / integrity findings
chunks.chunk_text:
XPoor and Insufficient Data Quality: Not collecting and retaining key information on variable annuity 
transactions, particularly in connection with exchange transactions; relying on processes for data collection 
and retention in situations where the volume of variable annuity transactions renders these processes 
ineffective; and failing to address inconsistencies in available data for variable annuities, as well as data 
formats and reporting processes.
 XIssuer Buyouts: Not reasonably supervising recommendations related to issuer buyout offers (e.g., registered 
representatives’ recommendations that investors surrender the contract to generate an exchange or new 
purchase) for compliance with FINRA Rule 2330 and Reg BI.
 XAdditional Deposits: Failing to evaluate and supervise registered representatives’ recommendations of 
additional deposits into existing VA contracts, including review of disclosure, any applicable surrender fees 
related to this transaction and rationale for the addition.
 XReasonably Available Alternatives: Pursuant to Reg BI, insufficient consideration of reasonably available 
alternatives to the recommended VA purchase, surrender or exchange. 
Effective Practices 
 XAutomated Surveillance: Using automated tools, exception reports and surveillance to review variable 
annuity exchanges; and implementing second-level supervision of supervisory reviews of exchange-related 
exception reports and account applications.
 XDetailed Rationales for VA Exchanges: Confirming that registered representatives’—and, where applicable, 
supervisory principals’—written rationales for variable annuity exchanges for each customer address the 
specific circumstances for each customer and do not replicate rationales provided for other customers; and 
requiring supervisory principals to verify the information in these rationales that registered representatives 
provide, including product fees, costs, rider benefits and existing product values.
 XClear Guidance for Retail Customers: Requiring registered representatives, when recommending 
variable annuities to retail customers, to provide them with clear, accessible materials that allow 
them to compare the fees, benefits lost or gained and surrender periods for different variable 
annuities.
 XReview Thresholds: Standardizing review thresholds for rates of variable annuity exchanges; and monitoring 
for emerging trends across registered representatives, customers, products and branches.
 XAutomated Data Supervision: Creating automated solutions to synthesize variable annuity data (including 
general pr
chunks.paraphrase_backup: Finding: poor and insufficient data quality — failing to collect and retain key VA transaction information and failing to address inconsistencies in available data, data formats, and reporting. Effective practice: confirm data integrity with carriers and third-party providers and address inconsistencies. This grounds the requirement that a suitability tool's answers (e.g., RightBridge fields) be internally consistent with the case notes and other documents.

chunks.section_ref: TX 28 TAC §§3.9701 et seq. (replacement); Ins. Code Ch. 1115
chunks.title: Texas — Replacement of life insurance and annuities

agent must obtain a statement signed by the consumer that meets the requirements of

(A) is substantially similar to the form specified in paragraph (2) of
this subsection;

chunks.paraphrase_backup: Texas replacement regulations require specific replacement notices and disclosures when an existing life insurance policy or annuity is replaced. Required replacement forms and the associated signatures are non-waivable. Texas Insurance Code §1115.054 provides a safe harbor recognizing compliance with FINRA suitability rules for certain annuity transactions, but does not waive the required state replacement forms.

chunks.section_ref: NAIC Model #613 — Replacement of Life Insurance and Annuities
chunks.title: NAIC #613 — Replacement notice signature/timing (absolute)
chunks.chunk_text:
application, a statement signed by both the applicant and the producer as to whether the 
applicant has existing policies or contracts. If the answer is “no,” the producer’s duties with 
respect to replacement are complete. 
 
B. If the applicant answered “yes” to the question regarding existing coverage referred to in 
Subsection A, the producer shall present and read to the applicant, not later than at the time of 
taking the application, a notice regarding replacements in the form as described in Appendix A 
or other substantially similar form approved by the commissioner. However, no approval shall 
be required when amendments to the notice are limited to the omission of references not 
applicable to the product being sold or replaced. The notice shall be signed by both the 
applicant and the producer attesting that the notice has been read aloud by the producer or 
that the applicant did not wish the notice to be read aloud (in which case the producer need not 
have read the notice aloud) and left with the applicant. 
 
C. The notice shall list all life insurance policies or annuities proposed to be replaced, properly 
identified by name of insurer, the insured or annuitant, and policy or contract number if 
available; and shall include a statement as to whether each policy or contract will be replaced or 
whether a policy will be used as a source of financing for the new policy or contract. If a policy 
or contract number has not been issued by the existing insurer, alternative identification, such 
as an application or receipt number, shall be listed. 

NAIC Model Laws, Regulations, Guidelines and Other Resources—2nd Quarter 2015 
 
© 2015 National Association of Insurance Commissioners MO-613-5 
D. In connection with a replacement transaction the producer shall leave with the applicant at the 
time an application for a new policy or contract is completed the original or a copy of all sales 
material. With respect to electronically presented sales material, it shall be provided to the 
policy or contract owner in printed form no later than at the time of policy or contract delivery. 
 
E. Except as provided in Section 5C, in connection with a replacement transaction the producer 
shall submit to the insurer to which an application for a policy or contract is presented, a copy of 
each document required by this section, a statement identifying any preprinted or electronically 
presented company approved sales materials used, and copies of any individualized sales 
materials, including any illustrations related to the specific policy or contract pur
chunks.paraphrase_backup: The replacement model requires the producer to present and read (or have the applicant read) the required replacement notice, and to obtain the applicant's and producer's signatures on the notice at or before the time the application is taken. This signature and timing requirement is absolute and not curable by later administrative amendment.

chunks.title: 1035 exchange — loan carryover / boot
chunks.chunk_text: A §1035 exchange is tax-free only to the extent no 'boot' is received. If an outstanding policy loan is extinguished at surrender rather than carried over to the receiving contract, the debt relief is treated as boot — a distribution taxable as ordinary income to the extent of gain. Therefore, on any 1035 with an outstanding loan, written confirmation from the ceding carrier that the loan will carry over is required to support the tax-free characterization before the exchange is approved.

chunks.section_ref: FFS Reg BI Supplement 326349 (structured/buffered VA description)
chunks.title: Buffered/structured VA — limited protection + caps
chunks.chunk_text: A structured/buffered variable annuity offers LIMITED downside protection, not full principal protection: the insurer absorbs the first portion of an annual loss (e.g., the first 10%) and the owner bears losses beyond that; these contracts also cap upside growth in a given period. Exiting a segment before maturity triggers an Interim Value Adjustment (IVA) that may be positive or negative and can reduce the value available for transfer. Notes must describe protection as limited (never full principal protection) and, where an IVA applies to the transaction, disclose it specifically.

chunks.title: Form 311883 — eSignature Terms & Conditions (DocuSign outside Docupace)
chunks.chunk_text: When DocuSign is used outside of Docupace, the completed eSignature Terms and Conditions Form (311883) must be included with the submission. A DocuSign certificate of completion does NOT satisfy this requirement and is not a substitute for the signed form.
chunks.stub_note: Confirmed from a real NIGO. Upload the actual 311883 form instructions to make this verbatim.

chunks.title: FFS internal procedures — PLACEHOLDER
chunks.chunk_text: PLACEHOLDER. The system cannot cite FFS-specific procedures it has not ingested. Upload the FFS compliance manual, WSPs, FCB bulletins, and Docupace submission procedures. Until then, any NIGO requirement that maps ONLY to an FFS procedure (not a FINRA/SEC/state rule) must be flagged as 'firm policy — not verifiable against uploaded FFS documents,' which is itself the correct and honest output.
chunks.stub_note: This is the single most valuable tier to populate. It is what lets the system say 'this is FFS policy, not FINRA' with a citation.

chunks.title: Carrier submission requirements — PLACEHOLDER
chunks.chunk_text: PLACEHOLDER per carrier (Pacific Life, Athene, MassMutual, Equitable, FNWL, etc.). Upload each carrier's submission and form requirements. Example real requirement already observed: Pacific Life accepts a 1035 exchange with an outstanding loan only on the condition that the ceding carrier permits the loan to carry over.
chunks.stub_note: Upload carrier submission guides to populate.
